# Tobacco giant's secret appearance at inquiry raises questions, health minister says

By political reporters [Stephanie Borys](https://www.abc.net.au/news/stephanie-borys/8477096), [Nicole Asher](https://www.abc.net.au/news/nicole-asher/6465000) and [Katie McAllister](https://www.abc.net.au/news/katie-mcallister/7132016)

- [Topic:Federal Government](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/federal-government)

18h ago18 hours agoTue 5 May 2026 at 1:32am

![A man with short hair, wearing a blue suit jacket, looking at the camera.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/21d784fd5de18fa99e13cfcd9ab338da?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3457&cropW=6147&xPos=0&yPos=1&width=862&height=485)

Mark Butler has questioned the inclusion of Philip Morris in an inquiry into illegal tobacco.  (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

## In short:

Mark Butler has raised concerns after Philip Morris was allowed to give testimony, in private, to a parliamentary inquiry into illegal tobacco.

The ABC understands the decision was made due to safety concerns for the Philip Morris representatives, but the move has angered health sector members who put their names and faces to their evidence.

## What's next?

The parliamentary inquiry, led by Liberal senator Leah Blyth, is due to release a report on the illegal tobacco market by the middle of this year.

The federal health minister and health groups are raising concerns about a secret appearance of cigarette giant Philip Morris at a parliamentary inquiry examining black market tobacco.

The inquiry, chaired by Liberal senator Leah Blyth, took a break yesterday afternoon with no explanation as to why.

After 15 minutes, the broadcast began again, with Greens senator Jordon Steele-John explaining Philip Morris representatives had just given evidence in private.

Philip Morris was not listed on yesterday's schedule of witnesses.

The ABC has confirmed two representatives appeared in person.

Health Minister Mark Butler said he was seeking more information about what had occurred.

> "If industry is giving evidence about ways in which public decisions will impact their profits, they should be answerable for that evidence,"

he said.

The ABC has been told by multiple sources the names of the Philip Morris witnesses were redacted due to safety concerns.

The illegal tobacco market is a growing problem for federal and state governments, as well as law enforcement agencies.

It has fuelled violence between crime cartels, with businesses being firebombed and a string of violent robberies.

Organised crime groups earned between $4.1 billion and $6.9 billion in profit from the illicit tobacco trade in 2024-25

More than [half of all the tobacco products now sold in Australia are illegal, according to a report by the Illicit Tobacco and E-Cigarette Commissioner](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-19/tobacco-crime-law-changes-for-illegal-vape-cigarette-market/106469048).

The parliament's inquiry into the illegal tobacco crisis is looking at what more can be done to address the problem.

## Health groups blast secrecy

Transcripts of the secret hearing will eventually be released, but Senator Blyth told the inquiry the names of the individual witnesses from Philip Morris would be redacted before then.

After wrapping up his own testimony in a session directly following the Philip Morris hearing, Lung Foundation Australia chief executive Mark Brooke raised concerns about the double standard.

"We put our names and faces out in front of organised crime gangs. How is that fair and reasonable?" Mr Brooke asked, before he was interrupted.

"Sorry, this is not a debating point, thank you for appearing," Senator Blyth cut in.

The ABC has asked Senator Blyth's office and the Committee Secretariat, which provides administrative support to the committee running the inquiry, why Philip Morris was not on the witness list and why the decision was made to keep their identities secret.

![A man in a white suit and black jacket wearing thick framed  glasses.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4c16611a38c9a9dcdff74a4c0f243aed?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=4497&cropW=6745&xPos=1&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Mark Brooke is questioning why tobacco industry representatives were allowed to hide their identities. (ABC News: Michael Lloyd)

Speaking outside the inquiry, Mr Brooke told the ABC he was appalled by the decision.

"Sixty-six Australians die every day from tobacco," he said.

"They and their families deserve significantly better than to have faceless executives … be invited by this inquiry … to give evidence in secret.

> "It is completely in the face of all good governance and transparency."

Australian Council on Smoking & Health chief executive Laura Hunter said a secret hearing was not acceptable.

"This is an industry that profits off addiction and disease and death. They should not be given any special privileges in this house," she said.

Cancer Council Australia chief executive Jacinta Reddan said politicians had a role to ensure accountability during inquiries.

"The lack of transparency just demonstrates the tobacco industry has not changed over the decades," she said.

"It is really concerning, and it would be concerning to all Australians that today for the first time in 16 years, big tobacco was given a platform behind closed doors," she said of the Commonwealth hearing.

## Philip Morris privacy request

The tobacco company was given similar treatment in February during a New South Wales inquiry into illegal tobacco, and in Victoria in 2024.

In Victoria, the reason given was "for safety" at the request of Philip Morris.

Transcripts from the NSW inquiry show Philip Morris representatives spoke about returning "nicotine markets back into the hands of regulators, not criminals", and lamented the high tobacco excise.

"We have to think about what are the market's long-term objectives around excise reform," Philip Morris's Witness B told the NSW inquiry.

"One of the really important objectives of it is to actually arrest the pace of consumer migration to the illicit market … and then actually increase participation in the legal tobacco market once again."

Witness B told the inquiry "an excise correction" would stem the flow of smokers opting for black-market tobacco, and "recover" legal consumption.

![Screen grab of a senate hearing featuring a woman with dark hear wearing a purple suit.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4f189eec9ab2e1861867691efc2bd0b5?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=271&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Leah Blyth says transcripts of the evidence given by Philip Morris will be released.  (Supplied: Senate/Illegal Tobacco Crisis Inquiry)

The health minister said he had told parliamentarians in recent days about the rules surrounding interacting with tobacco companies.

"I have reminded my parliamentary colleagues … about the obligations they have as public officials under the international conventions around tobacco control," Mr Butler said.

"That obligation includes not engaging with officials and employees of big tobacco.

"Although I have still got to get the full details, I am a little concerned about these reports I have heard."

Advice from the Department of Health states interactions with the tobacco industry "should be limited to what is necessary for public officials or agencies to enact effective tobacco control measures".

Philip Morris has been contacted for comment.

# Reserve Bank lifts interest rates by another 0.25pc to 4.35pc

By business reporter [Gareth Hutchens](https://www.abc.net.au/news/gareth-hutchens/12002796)

- [Topic:Interest Rates](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/interest-rates)

16h ago16 hours agoTue 5 May 2026 at 12:40am

![Reserve Bank of Australia](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3720bdd572b096bf25cba281b4222edf?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2385&cropW=4240&xPos=0&yPos=100&width=862&height=485)

Interest rates are now back at the same level as February 2025, before the Reserve Bank began its rate-cutting cycle. (ABC News: Daniel Irvine)

## In short:

The Reserve Bank has lifted interest rates by 0.25 percentage points to 4.35 per cent.

The cash rate target has now returned to its February 2025 level, which is where it sat before the RBA began last year's rate-cutting cycle.

## What's next?

The latest rate rise comes a week before the Albanese government delivers its federal budget on May 12.

The Reserve Bank has increased interest rates by 0.25 percentage points.

The new cash rate target is 4.35 per cent, up from 4.1 per cent.

Interest rates have now returned to the level they were in February 2025 before the RBA began its rate-cutting cycle last year, so last year's policy easing has been fully unwound.

Today's decision follows last week's news that [headline inflation accelerated sharply in March](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-29/monthly-inflation-surges-in-march-australia/106619740) as surging automotive fuel prices (+32.8 per cent in March), sparked by the war in the Middle East, pushed much higher inflation into Australia's economy.

The RBA has now lifted rates three times this year (in February, March and May), but RBA governor Michele Bullock said the rate rises would not stop higher inflation heading Australia's way from the war.

She said Australia was "staring down the barrel" of a very rough time in coming months as inflation rose, the economy slowed, and unemployment slowly picked up.

"These interest rate rises are not going to do anything for inflation in the next six months. That's done and dusted," Ms Bullock warned.

Today's rate rise comes a week before the Albanese government delivers its federal budget on May 12.

Financial markets had priced in a roughly 80 per cent chance of the RBA lifting rates today.

Today's policy decision was made by majority: eight members voted to increase the cash rate target by 25 basis points to 4.35 per cent, and one member voted to leave the cash rate target unchanged at 4.10 per cent.

Cash rate target, monthly average (%)

### Cash rate target, monthly average (%)

Chart explorer. Use arrow keys `↑`  `↓`  `←`  `→` to navigate.

Chart:ABC NewsSource:Reserve Bank of AustraliaGet the dataCreated with [Datawrapper](https://www.datawrapper.de/_/HlVXK)

## Why lift interest rates?

The war in the Middle East has caused the biggest global energy shock since the 1970s, with the price of crude oil surging to more than $US120 a barrel in recent weeks.

As a consequence, rising fuel prices have begun to push headline inflation sharply higher in Australia, and those higher cost pressures will seep into every expenditure class in the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and push underlying inflation higher in the coming months, too.

But economists say the RBA was already concerned about inflation before the war erupted in February.

They said this rate hike was necessary for the RBA to demonstrate its commitment to bringing inflation back down because inflation was already sitting above the RBA's target range of 2–3 per cent before the war broke out.

"All groups CPI" refers to headline inflation. The "trimmed mean" is the RBA's preferred measure of underlying inflation. Annual movements prior to April 2025 are calculated by comparing each quarter to the same quarter in the previous year. From April 2025 these movements are calculated by comparing each month to the same month in the previous year.

Chart:ABC NewsSource:Australian Bureau of StatisticsGet the dataCreated with [Datawrapper](https://www.datawrapper.de/_/2JVxa)

Earlier this week, former senior RBA official Jonathan Kearns, who left the bank three years ago to become chief economist at funds manager Challenger, said the RBA should lift rates this week to reduce the risk of stagflation.

Stagflation is a situation where economic activity is stagnating and inflation is high and rising, and damaging feedback loops develop between high inflation, poor growth, and employer and worker psychology.

"The term is usually applied to the 1970s when Australia and other rich countries had inflation frequently exceeding 10 per cent, combined with anaemic economic growth," [Dr Kearns wrote](https://www.afr.com/policy/economy/rba-must-raise-rates-to-lower-risk-of-stagflation-20260503-p5zt6s).

"On that basis, inflation of 4.6 per cent is well short of stagflation territory. But it is still too high for comfort.

"We may not have stagflation yet, and the risk may be small given our economy is less dependent on oil today.

"But the risk is real. A further cash rate rise that credibly brings inflation to target is the surest way the Reserve Bank can stop today's discomfort becoming tomorrow's trap," he wrote.

Economists have also been pointing to the recent collapse in business and consumer confidence in Australia.

The latest NAB business survey found that business confidence suffered its second-largest monthly decline in the survey's history in March, in an "ominous sign" for business activity in the coming months.

Economists say it is extremely important to keep a handle on inflation in this environment to try to support business confidence.

![Consumer and business confidence March and April 2026](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/be7becd50b8cd266db5fed48b7c3a60a?src)

(Source: Westpac economist Luka Belobradjdic, Australian Business Conditions and Confidence, March \[April 14, 2026\])

Last week, Deutsche Bank chief economist Phil O'Donaghoe said the RBA could only use interest rates to manage demand, and it would make little difference to the trajectory for inflation over the coming months if the cash rate were 4.35 per cent or 10 per cent.

"\[But\] the best the RBA can do is demonstrate to price and wage setters its commitment to the inflation target by taking rates back to the post-COVID peak of 4.35 per cent," he wrote.

He said if the RBA did lift rates today, he thought it would be the last rate rise for 2026.

## RBA forecasting slower growth, rising inflation and unemployment

The RBA has published updated forecasts in its quarterly [Statement on Monetary Policy (SOMP)](https://www.rba.gov.au/publications/smp/2026/may/pdf/statement-on-monetary-policy-2026-05.pdf).

It is now officially forecasting headline inflation to peak at 4.8 per cent in the middle of this year (it is now 4.6 per cent), with underlying "trimmed mean" inflation peaking at 3.8 per cent in the middle of this year (up from 3.3 per cent).

It is forecasting the annual rate of economic growth to halve from 2.6 per cent currently to 1.3 per cent by the end of this year.

And it expects the unemployment rate to rise over the next two years from 4.3 per cent to 4.7 per cent.

But it has also modelled two adverse scenarios that could potentially occur in Australia's economy if the Middle East conflict is longer lasting, including an extended closure of the Strait of Hormuz, and even worse damage occurs to energy infrastructure and production in the Middle East.

In both scenarios, the RBA assumes energy prices will rise very sharply in the near term and remain elevated over the forecast period.

But the two scenarios are differentiated by the degree to which total (aggregate) demand in Australia is affected by a more protracted conflict.

Inflation and unemployment get increasingly worse in Australia as the war drags on.

![RBA adverse scenarios](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/9e1aa718ef8764dfe4a7c6b95f2e4b62?src)

(Source: Reserve Bank of Australia, Statement on Monetary Policy, May 2026, page. 62)

## RBA governor warns of tough times ahead as incomes take a hit

In her press conference after the RBA Board meeting today, Ms Bullock said the global energy shock had already hit the real incomes of Australians and people were feeling poorer for it.

She said people would probably start asking for pay rises to deal with higher inflation, and the RBA would have to manage whatever happened.

> "I fully expect that people will try to make up their real wages, the hit they've taken from inflation, by asking for higher wages,"

she said.

"The extent to which they can achieve them depends on the tightness of the labour market, so it depends on how much bargaining power they have, and ... there probably will be some success there.

"But what I don't want is for \[higher inflation\] to become ingrained in expectations going forward."

# Romania's pro-EU government collapses after no-confidence vote

- [Topic:World Politics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/world-politics)

4h ago4 hours agoTue 5 May 2026 at 10:21am

![A middle aged man wearing a suit speaking in a parliament.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/07cbf8c121eac18b503f9939f246babc?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1738&cropW=3090&xPos=0&yPos=161&width=862&height=485)

Ousted Romanian PM Ilie Bolojan is accusing opposition parties of lacking an alternative plan to tackle the country's deficit problems. (Reuters: Inquam Photos/Octav Ganea)

## In short:

Romania's pro-EU coalition government led by Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan has collapsed after losing a confidence vote in parliament.

The country's currency fell to a record low against the Euro amid the instability, with fears that Bucharest may not be able to contain the worst budget deficit in the European Union.

## What's next?

The country's president, Nicusor Dan, will invite parties to try to reform a coalition under a new leader.

Romania's parliament has toppled Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan's pro-EU government in a no-confidence vote, putting at risk the country's sovereign debt ratings, its access to EU funds and the stability of its currency.

Mr Bolojan has led a minority government since late April when the Social Democrats — the largest party in parliament — called for his resignation and then walked out of the four-party coalition and teamed up with the far-right opposition to file a no-confidence vote.

Tuesday's no-confidence motion garnered 281 votes, above the 233 needed to pass, the official parliamentary count showed.

Although a snap election looks unlikely, financial markets are concerned that the turbulence could mean Bucharest wavers in its commitment to narrowing the European Union's biggest budget deficit.

![A line of people beside a large Orthodox church on a sunny day.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/36cfa81b62a654e2309daad01a08006b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Romania has the largest budget deficit of any nation in the European Union. (Reuters: Andreea Campeanu)

Romania's leu currency fell to a record low against the euro ahead of Tuesday's vote.

The current coalition came to power 10 months ago with a view to containing the gains of the far right after a series of polarising elections, and it had begun to reduce the deficit, narrowly avoiding a ratings downgrade from the last rung of investment grade.

But the Social Democrats — without whom a pro-EU majority cannot be achieved — have repeatedly clashed with Mr Bolojan as his austerity measures have hit their voters and patronage networks, while their popular support has bled away to the far right.

Nevertheless, opinion polls show Mr Bolojan is the most popular politician in the ruling coalition.

Speaking before the vote, Mr Bolojan accused the parties seeking to topple his government of lacking any coherent alternative vision for the country.

"Can anyone say how Romania will function from tomorrow, do you have a plan?" Mr Bolojan asked politicians before the vote.

"Romanians will understand that you can govern differently, with respect for public money, and you cannot undo that."

Romania's next parliamentary election is not due until 2028.

It has never held an early election and analysts say the likelihood of one now is small as the opposition hard-right Alliance for Uniting Romanians (AUR) leads in opinion polls.

Centrist President Nicusor Dan, who nominates the prime minister, is now expected to invite parties for negotiations and attempt to rebuild the four-party pro-EU coalition under a different member of Mr Bolojan's Liberals or perhaps a technocrat as prime minister.

The Social Democrats (PSD) have often said they would rejoin a pro-EU coalition under a different premier.

Mr Bolojan's party has so far ruled out collaborating with the Social Democrats again, though some senior party members have pushed for reconciliation.

"There is life after the no-confidence vote," PSD leader Sorin Grindeanu told reporters.

"We want to keep broadly this coalition."

**Reuters**

# Fuel prices rising in the US as costs stabilise in Australia

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f9f88bb67d2b86488ae2dc26108ecc93?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3024&cropW=3024&xPos=0&yPos=264&width=160&height=160)\\

A\\

By Ahmed Yussuf](https://www.abc.net.au/news/ahmed-yussuf/13177774)
- [Topic:Petrol Prices](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/petrol-prices)

2h ago2 hours agoTue 5 May 2026 at 5:08pm

![Fuel prices advertised at more than $2.50 a litre at Ampol petrol station.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/18147d3c900fa185bc2622decdbcea5b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1447&cropW=2573&xPos=0&yPos=135&width=862&height=485)

Fuel prices in Australia have started to moderate but have reached an almost four-year high in the United States. (ABC News: Timothy Ailwood)

## In short:

Fuel prices in Australia have fallen in recent weeks, with the cost of unleaded almost where it was before the US-Israeli war on Iran.

But the United States has experienced an increase as fuel prices reach an almost four-year high.

## What's next?

Some experts say fuel prices are likely to rise again in June with the Strait of Hormuz still closed and the fuel excise cut ending next month.

Fuel prices in the United States hit their highest levels in almost four years at the end of April, just as price rises in Australia started to moderate.

The average price for 95 octane unleaded petrol in the US has increased by more than 40 per cent since February.

The US-Israeli war on Iran, and subsequent disruption of oil exports through the Strait of Hormuz, sent benchmark prices for oil soaring in late February.

As a leading global oil producer and a major fuel refiner, the US was able to weather the initial price shock for longer than other countries, according to experts.

Here's how Australia and the US compare when it comes to the prices at the bowser.

## Fuel prices in Australia and the US

Australian petrol prices have almost returned to pre-conflict levels.

When the conflict began on February 28, the average price of unleaded 91 was 181.5 cents a litre.

Now, the average price for a litre of unleaded is 185.4 cents.

But prices now reflect the federal government's [halving of the fuel excise](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-30/fuel-excise-halved-three-months-petrol-diesel/106510432) [,](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-05-06/fuel-prices-rising-in-us-as-costs-stabilise-in-australia/106630042) which reduced the cost of petrol and diesel by 26.3 cents a litre for three months.

## What experts say about the diverging gap

University of Sydney supply chain expert Ben Fahimnia said Australian petrol prices moved in cycles, with sharp peaks followed by a slow discounting phase.

"Many cities have recently been in this downward phase, which means prices at the pump can fall even when upstream cost pressures are building," he said.

"This behaviour is largely driven by local competitive dynamics rather than the immediate transmission of input cost changes to retail prices."

Economist Peter Robertson said the US had been shielded from the initial price shock because it is one of the world's leading oil producers, while Australia largely relied on imported fuel.

"So when the crisis first broke out, there was a big reaction in Australia, and prices were tied into the Singapore refineries, Malaysia refineries and Asia markets more generally," he said.

"The concern wasn't there in the United States. They weren't reliant on the oil imports. And so you didn't get a big price spike in the US the way we did in Australia."

![An industrial plant](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/13f6755fea773bf8c279a2820e575543?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=85&width=862&height=575)

Ampol's Lytton Refinery in Brisbane is one of only two active refineries in Australia. (AAP: Darren England)

Lurion De Mello, from Macquarie University's Transforming Energy Markets Centre, said storage capacity in the US also had stopped fuel prices from spiking, but that was beginning to change.

"I think the supply is getting tight even for the biggest world exporter, and that's putting pressure on fuel prices over there to go up," Dr De Mello said.

University of Sydney associate professor David Ubilava said the fuel excise reduction had also helped curb petrol prices in Australia.

"Why we are seemingly moving in the opposite direction of the US is that we are on this downward sloping part of the cycle," Dr Ubilava said.

"In the US, we are pretty much seeing the effect of this war and then no mitigation of it due to tax rate changes."

## What's next for fuel prices?

Retail fuel prices in Australia are linked to refined product benchmarks such as Singapore Mogas 95, according to Professor Fahimnia.

But there is a seven- to 14-day lag before international prices are reflected at the local bowser.

"This lag arises due to contracting structures and distribution timelines," he said.

"As a result, current retail prices may still reflect earlier, lower input costs, with upward pressure only materialising in subsequent weeks."

Dr De Mello said the dip in prices in April reflected an increase in fuel prices in Australia compared to the same time the previous year.

But he said prices were likely to increase in June, partly due to the fuel excise cut expiring and the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

"There's almost 2 billion barrels of oil taken out of the market," he said.

"If the alternative sources of oil from the other oil fields outside of the Middle East, if they can't keep up with the extra demand, we could see a small reversal in petrol prices and diesel prices heading into June."

The US-Israeli war on Iran has already had an effect on [inflation in Australia](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-29/monthly-inflation-surges-in-march-australia/106619740), with it rising by almost a percentage point to 4.6 per cent in March.

It was largely driven by automotive fuel prices, which rose by more than 30 per cent.

# New Hilton hotel announced for Adelaide adds up to 15 under construction

- [![A headshot of a smiling woman with a dark background](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/25e1b354c8f3d387359ef3b0d3a50ea0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=3333&xPos=188&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
S\\
\\
By Sophie Holder](https://www.abc.net.au/news/sophie-holder/101859270)
- [By Eugene Boisvert](https://www.abc.net.au/news/eugene-boisvert/9902214)
- [Topic:Urban Development and Planning](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/urban-development-and-planning)

3h ago3 hours agoTue 5 May 2026 at 4:08pm

![A tall building with the word Hilton on the podium](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/21b9dd96bcc269e11d614f2b80aa5010?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=600&cropW=800&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=647)

A design for the new Hilton East End Adelaide hotel on Pirie Street. (Supplied: Hilton Australasia)

## In short:

Hilton and the premier say major events are boosting interest in Adelaide's East End.

Industry leaders say there is demand for more hotel rooms from tourists and businesspeople.

## What's next?

Fifteen new hotels are planned for the Adelaide CBD.

The latest major hotel project announced for Adelaide has added to a looming accommodation construction boom in the CBD, but the industry is confident there will be more than enough visitors to the city to fill the thousands of new rooms in the pipeline.

The new Hilton development in the city's East End, announced yesterday, will be a 27-storey, 251-room hotel.

It is one of 15 new hotels planned for Adelaide's CBD, with a long list of other developments either being planned or already under construction.

If all of those developments proceed, they would add more than 2,000 hotel rooms to the CBD.

![a building tower being erected with blue sky in background and cars on road in front](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ac87cc12d09bd170124a4a59d79eb5ca?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2265&cropW=3398&xPos=223&yPos=625&width=862&height=575)

The Treehouse Hotel above the Market Square development is set to open by the end of the year. (891 ABC Adelaide: Troy Sincock)

Australian Hotels Association SA chief executive Anna Moeller said she was confident there were enough tourists for the number of new rooms that would be available because occupancy rates were "holding firm".

"Certainly, there's sufficient demand, and I think with the influx of the additional major events that have attracted not just intrastate but interstate and international visitors, then that's the thing that supports that growth in rooms," she said.

> "We can see that occupancy has been sustained, and therefore, yes, the growth is being supported."

![A woman in a black and white striped dress poses on a balcony](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e6fcc51e9e1d374738d752e943c11f9c?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2747&cropW=4121&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Anna Moeller from the Australian Hotels Association says rooms will be filled. (ABC News: Ashlin Blieschke)

## 'Sufficient demand' for rooms

Other hotels set to open in the Adelaide CBD this year include the 248-room Treehouse Hotel above the Market Square development on Grote Street, the Little National Hotel on North Terrace and the Crystalbrook Sam Hotel on Halifax Street.

The Oakwood Premier Adelaide is due to open on Wright Street next year, along with the Veriu serviced-apartment project at the corner of King William Street and Grenfell Street.

Westin announced in 2023 that it would occupy 19 floors of the [Keystone tower](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-05-25/adelaide-skyline-growing-but-construction-workers-needed/102356086) approved for North Terrace but only preliminary work has started on the site.

![A hotel room with a view of a city](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/225e28967ca8049dec4d375bb212c763?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=960&cropW=1440&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

What a hotel room at the Little National Adelaide will look like. (Supplied: Little National)

Ms Moeller said despite the growth, there was not a "glut" of rooms.

"There's certainly not hotels that are losing the sale of rooms because of new inventory coming onto the market," she said.

The 285-room Adelaide Marriott Hotel opened in 2024 in a new 15-storey building that is integrated with the 154-year-old former General Post Office building.

![Street view of Adelaide's Marriott hotel showing new and old buildings.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/8c104e9332f65bf95d8af6fa5c204100?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=861&cropW=1292&xPos=0&yPos=1&width=862&height=575)

The Adelaide Marriott Hotel involved a redevelopment of the old GPO building. (Supplied)

The Sofitel opened in 2021, along with the Hotel Indigo Adelaide Markets and the Majestic M Suites in North Adelaide.

In 2020, new hotels included Eos by SkyCity above the Adelaide Casino, the Oval Hotel at Adelaide Oval and the Crowne Plaza on Frome Street.

## Business traveller focus

Both Ms Moeller and Daisy Miller, who has just taken over the helm at the Tourism Industry Council South Australia, agreed that business travellers were also a significant part of tourism in the state, and especially the CBD.

![A group of people including a woman wearing a green dress sitting down in the front row](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/786a045df18e045d9abfa11e5e9cfbed?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=562&cropW=562&xPos=646&yPos=533&width=862&height=862)

Tourism Industry Council South Australia chief executive Daisy Miller. (Supplied: TiCSA)

"We do see that business travellers, and convention attendees, spend more money in the location," Ms Miller said.

"This hotel investment that we're seeing is also about that investment that's been made, not just in those major events which are obviously huge drawcards and show the world we're a place where exciting things happen, but we're also a place where business deals are done.

"There's going to continue to be a pipeline of bread-and-butter events, those events that happen between Monday to Friday, rather than Friday and Sunday."

Ms Miller said those events showed a confidence in South Australia being a place where things happen.

She said there was "absolutely" demand for the number of hotel rooms in Adelaide, and that the investments should bring confidence for other businesses.

New Adelaide hotels

### New Adelaide hotels

Showing a low-resolution version of the map. Make sure your browser supports WebGL to see the full version.

![New Adelaide hotels](https://datawrapper.dwcdn.net/hQnQe/plain.png)

"These big major hotel chains have got confidence that being here is the right business move for them," she said.

"But I also think that even if we were to see those numbers drop a little bit as more supply comes on … it will make that hotel accommodation more accessible to more people, to increase the number of people coming for things like Gather Round.

"Which is good again for our small businesses both in the CBD and the regions, as people disperse from those events to explore what we've got to offer."

![A group of people looking at a large piece of paper near city buildings](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ae37ce032415e4605ea3c51d0a88988b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=51&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Premier Peter Malinauskas at the launch of the Hilton East End Adelaide hotel with Tushar Raniga from Hilton (left), developer Eric Luk and Tourism Minister Emily Bourke. (ABC News)

Hilton Australasia director of development Tushar Raniga said the project "reflects our confidence in Adelaide, South Australia's long-term growth as a destination for business, tourism, culture and major events".

The new Hilton is set to open in 2031, creating 700 direct jobs during construction and 300 ongoing hospitality roles.

## Suburbs join boom

In Adelaide's suburbs, the 77-room Hotel Panorama is set to open on Friday, [replacing the Tonsley Hotel](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-05-12/how-the-north-south-corridor-is-changing-adelaide/105237136) — also owned by the Hurley Hotel Group — that was demolished in 2024 to make way for the Torrens to Darlington underground motorway project.

![A design for a five-store hotel](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d37309255e87b7953d63f07d076a076a?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=720&cropW=1080&xPos=100&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The Hotel Panorama is set to open on Goodwood Road on Friday. (Supplied)

The same company also revamped the nearby Marion Hotel with accommodation in 2019.

Other hotel developments outside the CBD where work is underway include the Marine and Harbours Hotel in Port Adelaide and the Alexandra Terrace hotel in Glenelg.

The George Hotel opened in Glenelg last year and the Urban Rest Hackney Hotel earlier this year.

# AUDIO: Jefferson Lewis excused from court \| ABC News Top Stories

- [News Briefings Top Stories audio](https://www.abc.net.au/news/audio/news-briefings-audio-top-stories/)

21h ago21 hours agoMon 4 May 2026 at 10:04pm

Has Audio Duration: 1 minute 34 seconds.

Share options- [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.abc.net.au%2Fnews%2F2026-05-05%2Fjefferson-lewis-excused-from-court%2F106643010%3Futm_content%3Dfacebook%26utm_medium%3Dcontent_shared "Share by Facebook")
- [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.abc.net.au%2Fnews%2F2026-05-05%2Fjefferson-lewis-excused-from-court%2F106643010%3Futm_content%3Dlinked-in%26utm_medium%3Dcontent_shared "Share by LinkedIn")
- [X (formerly Twitter)](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=Share+on&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.abc.net.au%2Fnews%2F2026-05-05%2Fjefferson-lewis-excused-from-court%2F106643010%3Futm_content%3Dtwitter%26utm_medium%3Dcontent_shared&via=ABCaustralia "Share by X (formerly Twitter)")

The man accused of murdering five-year-old Kumanjayi Little Baby has been excused from appearing in court in the Northern Territory.

Jefferson Lewis was expected to face Alice Springs Local Court this morning via a video link from Darwin, but his lawyer requested he be excused.

Mr Lewis is charged with one count of murder and two other charges that cannot be disclosed for legal reasons.

The prosecution's requested a lengthy adjournment, with the case expected to return to court on July 30.

The Opposition is accusing the Prime Minister of being a "liar", as the government prepares to unveil major changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing in next week's budget.

While the exact details are yet to be confirmed, the government is laying the groundwork by arguing it needs to fix intergenerational inequity to help younger Australians wanting to enter the housing market.

Opposition Leader Angus Taylor says during last year's election campaign, Labor repeatedly promised it wouldn't touch negative gearing.

The Reserve Bank will walk a careful line between controlling inflation and steering the country away from a recession when it hands down its rates decision this afternoon.

Inflation jumped for the first quarter of this year, leading markets to factor in an 82% chance of an increase to 4.35%.

But some experts argue hiking rates too much further could be a wet blanket for the economy as the Middle East conflict continues.

# Iran's mosquito fleet a 'weapon of mass disruption' in Strait of Hormuz

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/b260245fb07b562cd65fd60834247f91?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1738&cropW=1738&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
A\\
\\
By Annika Burgess](https://www.abc.net.au/news/annika-burgess/13489366)
- [Topic:Armed Conflict](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/armed-conflict)

28m ago28 minutes agoTue 5 May 2026 at 7:31pm

![A helicopter hovers over three speed boats in the water. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/5790fd7ef884884d42e8277456999168?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2172&cropW=3861&xPos=0&yPos=102&width=862&height=485)

A US Navy helicopter hovers over Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps speed boats.

(Reuters: Hamad I Mohammed)

## In short:

Despite losing much of its conventional navy, Iran is using its so-called mosquito fleet of small attack boats to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

With potentially thousands in hiding, Iran is using swarms of boats to surround and attack merchant vessels.

## What's next?

The US is beefing up air power over the strait, claiming to have recently sunk at least six small Iranian boats with Seahawk helicopters.

Lurking in sea caves, ready to swarm, Iran's so-called mosquito fleet of small, speedy attack boats has become an increasingly aggressive nuisance in the Strait of Hormuz.

President Donald Trump claims the United States has "completely obliterated" the majority of the Iranian navy.

He has boasted about destroying submarines and sinking several warships, saying the rest "will soon be floating at the bottom of the sea".

But Iran does [not only rely on big ships and a traditional navy](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-21/the-battle-over-the-strait-of-hormuz-iran-war-tactics/106467826) to disrupt global oil shipping and maintain its blockade on the strait.

It also has an unconventional force operating in the seas, built around dispersion, speed and volume.

With possibly thousands of weapons-laden speedboats hidden away, fleets are launched en masse to quickly surround and attack larger vessels.

"They call them 'mosquito fleets' because they're small and annoying — and they hit," former Pentagon official and Atlantic Council fellow Alex Plitsas told New York Post.

"But they're enough to bite and be obnoxious."

## How big is the bite?

Operated by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy (IRGCN), the mosquito fleet was established in the 80s as a second asymmetric navy.

Instead of operating large conventional vessels, it used small civilian speedboats mounted with rocket-propelled grenades or machine guns.

Today, the attack boats have become more bespoke, often carrying missiles and used [with mini submarines and drone warfare.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-04/iran-drone-boats-attack-oil-tankers-strait-hormuz-gulf-oman/106408958)

Jennifer Parker, a former naval officer and maritime security expert at The Australian National University National Security College, said the mosquito fleets had a long history of confrontation in the strait and Persian Gulf.

"They're definitely a threat," she told the ABC.

"They traditionally come out and harass vessels and merchant ships."

![Two large vessels sit by a dock in the background as two men sit by the water in the foreground.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/38a2bab6ef37578f6ddb9a177ac5b163?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3302&cropW=4953&xPos=5&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Tankers were left stranded as Iran threatened to attack any ships that moved through the strait during the conflict.  (Reuters: Benoit Tessier)

Ms Parker had her own encounter with the Iranian attack boats when serving in the region in 2008.

"They would threaten you, they'd come at you at speed, they'd point their weapons at you … they'd steer really close to the vessel," she said.

## Swarms at sea

The heavily armed speedboats were recently used to swarm and open fire on Indian-flagged tankers near the strait.

Often, the small vessels are launched from multiple directions at high speeds, which can saturate surveillance and defensive systems.

Ms Parker said merchant shipping vessels generally had no way of defending against the mosquito fleets to avoid capture.

"They don't generally employ guards to shoot these vessels (speedboats), and they certainly don't have machine guns," she said.

"Merchant vessels go quite slow, so all they can do is try and manoeuvre, but they will probably be captured."

## Potentially thousands in hiding

Analysts estimate the IRGCN could have anywhere between hundreds to thousands of small attack boats.

"The reason it's hard to understand how many they have is because a lot of them are kept in cavernous bunkers under the mountains," Ms Parker said.

Iran's judiciary chief, Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei, said on X that "the IRGC's mosquito fleet, with speedboats and drones, lies in wait from the sea caves of Faror Island for the American aggressor warships, ready to saturate their air defences and bring utter ruin upon the invaders".

Adrian Blomfield, a senior foreign correspondent at The Telegraph, has been reporting from the Strait of Hormuz.

He said the mosquito fleets could also blend in with the "enormous amount of traffic" moving around the strait, with hundreds of speedboats frequently bouncing across the water.

"You can see just how easy it is for the IRGC speedboats to hide in plain sight, whether that's for scouting operations, mining operations, or boarding operations," he said in an interview with France 24.

He said rather than using "weapons of mass destruction", the mosquito fleet was enabling Iran to control the strait with "a weapon of mass disruption".

## US covers skies over strait

Mr Trump has dismissed the capabilities of Iran's asymmetric navy, saying the US was not firing at the "fast attack ships" because he did not consider them to be "much of a threat".

But analysts have observed the mosquito fleet was becoming increasingly active in recent weeks.

Before the war, about 20 per cent of the world's oil flowed through the strait.

Iran's blockade of the waterway and the US military's subsequent closure of Iranian ports in the Gulf of Oman have sent energy prices skyrocketing.

![Sun setting behind a mountain as ships and boats sit in the ocean.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2b323eb9da2118a584d399ba92669e58?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=4220&xPos=390&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The US and Iran are wrestling for control over the Strait of Hormuz with duelling maritime blockades. (Reuters)

The use of small attack boats swarming from various directions in a narrow area like the strait was putting additional pressure on US naval forces.

"A couple of drones and a couple of small boats … have been able to choke the world's largest strategic waterway at risk without actually permanently closing it — and wreaking havoc on the markets," Mr Plitsas from the Atlantic Council said.

US Navy Seahawk helicopters destroyed six Iranian small boats on Monday, local time, as part of ongoing efforts to reopen the strait to commercial vessels, according to Admiral Brad Cooper, head of US Central Command.

Iranian media rejected Mr Trump's claims the US attacked the IRGC speedboats.

The speedboats can be difficult for vessels to defend against, but Ms Parker said they were vulnerable to helicopter attacks.

Mr Trump announced a new "Project Freedom" plan to help stuck ships travel through the strait, which would involve hundreds of land and sea-based aircraft.

"Part of that tactic, I suspect, is to get them further away from Iran's coast so they are less susceptible to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy," Ms Parker said.

"And then saturate the air with helicopters predominantly, but also fixed-wing aircraft, that can then respond to and take out these Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy vessels."

The commencement of "Project Freedom" has been paused for a short time in order to see whether a "complete and final agreement with representatives of Iran" can be finalised and signed, Mr Trump posted on his Truth Social social media site on Wednesday.

The blockade of the strait would remain in full force and effect, he added.

He said rather than using "weapons of mass destruction", the mosquito fleet was enabling Iran to control the strait with "a weapon of mass disruption".

## US covers skies over strait

Mr Trump has dismissed the capabilities of Iran's asymmetric navy, saying the US was not firing at the "fast attack ships" because he did not consider them to be "much of a threat".

But analysts have observed the mosquito fleet was becoming increasingly active in recent weeks.

Before the war, about 20 per cent of the world's oil flowed through the strait.

Iran's blockade of the waterway and the US military's subsequent closure of Iranian ports in the Gulf of Oman have sent energy prices skyrocketing.

![Sun setting behind a mountain as ships and boats sit in the ocean.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2b323eb9da2118a584d399ba92669e58?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=4220&xPos=390&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The US and Iran are wrestling for control over the Strait of Hormuz with duelling maritime blockades. (Reuters)

The use of small attack boats swarming from various directions in a narrow area like the strait was putting additional pressure on US naval forces.

"A couple of drones and a couple of small boats … have been able to choke the world's largest strategic waterway at risk without actually permanently closing it — and wreaking havoc on the markets," Mr Plitsas from the Atlantic Council said.

US Navy Seahawk helicopters destroyed six Iranian small boats on Monday, local time, as part of ongoing efforts to reopen the strait to commercial vessels, according to Admiral Brad Cooper, head of US Central Command.

Iranian media rejected Mr Trump's claims the US attacked the IRGC speedboats.

The speedboats can be difficult for vessels to defend against, but Ms Parker said they were vulnerable to helicopter attacks.

Mr Trump announced a new "Project Freedom" plan to help stuck ships travel through the strait, which would involve hundreds of land and sea-based aircraft.

"Part of that tactic, I suspect, is to get them further away from Iran's coast so they are less susceptible to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy," Ms Parker said.

"And then saturate the air with helicopters predominantly, but also fixed-wing aircraft, that can then respond to and take out these Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy vessels."

The commencement of "Project Freedom" has been paused for a short time in order to see whether a "complete and final agreement with representatives of Iran" can be finalised and signed, Mr Trump posted on his Truth Social social media site on Wednesday.

The blockade of the strait would remain in full force and effect, he added.
